text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY - FOLLOW-UP VISIT NOTE
Metastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer

ATTENDING: Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established patient follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with germline BRCA2-mutant metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) presenting for follow-up after completing Cycle 4 of carboplatin (AUC 4) plus docetaxel (55 mg/m2, dose-reduced). He reports worsening fatigue, increasing difficulty with ambulation due to peripheral neuropathy, and is accompanied today by his daughter and son-in-law.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Kowalski is a well-known patient to our service with a lengthy and complex oncologic course. Briefly, he was initially diagnosed approximately three years ago with Stage IVB (T3bN1M1b) Gleason 9 (5+4) Grade Group 5 prostatic adenocarcinoma with bone metastases (L3, L4, right iliac crest, right sacral ala) and right pelvic lymphadenopathy at an initial PSA of 187.4 ng/mL. He was found to carry a germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT). Somatic tumor profiling demonstrated BRCA2 biallelic loss, AR amplification, TP53 p.R175H, RB1 loss, and PTEN loss -- a biologically aggressive phenotype.

He received first-line ADT + abiraterone/prednisone for approximately 24 months with an excellent initial PSA response (nadir 0.8 ng/mL) before developing castration-resistant progression (PSA rising to 8.6 ng/mL, castrate testosterone). He was transitioned to enzalutamide in the second-line setting, achieving a modest PSA response (nadir 1.9 ng/mL) before progressing at approximately 7 months with new bone metastases on bone scan and PSMA PET/CT confirming new pelvic lymph node involvement. Consistent with his BRCA2 biallelic loss, he was transitioned to olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID in the third-line setting, achieving a 69% PSA decline initially. However, his course on olaparib was complicated by severe, recurrent myelosuppression requiring two packed RBC transfusions, dose reductions (to 200 mg BID, then 150 mg BID), and ultimately progressive disease at 11 months with new visceral metastases (liver segment VII, left lower lobe lung nodule), new bone lesions, and a PSA of 31.7 ng/mL. Olaparib was discontinued.

Given visceral involvement and concern for visceral crisis, he was initiated on carboplatin AUC 4 / docetaxel 60 mg/m2 every 21 days with G-CSF (filgrastim) prophylaxis. Docetaxel was dose-reduced to 55 mg/m2 after Cycle 1 due to Grade 3 neutropenia and worsening peripheral neuropathy (background Grade 1 neuropathy likely from prior taxane exposure at initial diagnosis -- actually, this was his first taxane exposure, and he developed Grade 2 neuropathy after Cycle 1). After 2 cycles, restaging CT showed a partial response in the hepatic lesion (1.4 cm to 0.8 cm) and a declining PSA to 18.4 ng/mL. After 3 cycles, PSA further declined to 12.1 ng/mL (62% reduction from pre-chemo peak of 31.7) and he was tolerating the regimen with the dose reduction in place, though Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy was documented in bilateral feet and hands.

He now presents today for his post-Cycle 4 assessment. He reports that he has had a more difficult time since the last infusion. He notes significant fatigue limiting his daily activities -- he can no longer walk to the mailbox without stopping to rest, and his daughter notes he spends most of the day in a recliner. He has been needing help with dressing and bathing over the past 2-3 weeks, which is new. He continues to have bilateral foot numbness extending to mid-calf bilaterally, with new numbness and tingling in the fingertips bilaterally, and reports difficulty with fine motor tasks (buttoning shirts, holding utensils). He had a near-fall last week reaching for a glass of water. He endorses ongoing mild-to-moderate diffuse bone pain managed with scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg TID and hydrocodone/acetaminophen 5/325 mg as needed (using approximately 2-3 tabs per day). Appetite remains reduced; he estimates he has lost an additional 3-4 lbs since his last visit (cumulative approximately 8-11 lbs over the past 3 months). He denies fevers, chills, or productive cough. No new RUQ pain (previously resolved). No hemoptysis or new neurologic symptoms beyond peripheral neuropathy. Bowel and bladder function intact. He has not required a blood transfusion since approximately 6 weeks ago.

He and his family express that quality of life is paramount. He wants to "keep fighting" if there is a reasonable option, but does not want to be "in the hospital all the time." His daughter raises the question of whether there is "something gentler" that could still help.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: Fatigue (significant, Grade 2-3), bilateral peripheral neuropathy feet and hands (Grade 2-3), near-fall event, diffuse bone pain (partially controlled), reduced appetite, weight loss (8-11 lbs over 3 months), dyspnea on exertion (mild, Grade 1). Alopecia (Grade 2, patient aware, wearing cap).
Negative: No fever, no chills, no rigors. No chest pain or palpitations. No new back pain beyond baseline. No bowel changes. No urinary obstruction or hematuria. No confusion, no seizures, no focal neurological deficits beyond peripheral neuropathy. No bleeding or easy bruising noted by patient. No nausea or vomiting at present (was Grade 2 during infusion week, now resolved).

---

MEDICATIONS (current):
1. Leuprolide acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (last injection approximately 6 weeks ago, next due in 6 weeks) -- continued for testosterone castration
2. Docetaxel 55 mg/m2 IV day 1 q21 days (most recent Cycle 4 administered approximately 2 weeks ago)
3. Carboplatin AUC 4 IV day 1 q21 days (Cycle 4 administered with docetaxel)
4. Filgrastim (G-CSF) 300 mcg SC days 3-10 each cycle (prophylactic; patient self-administering)
5. Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg SC every 4 weeks (last dose approximately 3 weeks ago)
6. Dexamethasone 8 mg IV pre-chemotherapy (infusion day only)
7. Ondansetron 8 mg PO TID PRN nausea (has 7-day supply after each infusion)
8. Prochlorperazine 10 mg PO q6h PRN breakthrough nausea
9. Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO TID (scheduled)
10. Hydrocodone/acetaminophen 5/325 mg PO q6h PRN moderate-severe pain
11. Calcium carbonate 500 mg PO daily (with vitamin D 400 IU)
12. Darbepoetin alfa (Aranesp) 500 mcg SC every 3 weeks (for chronic anemia management; last dose 10 days ago)
13. Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily (GI protection)
14. Docusate sodium 100 mg PO BID (bowel regimen given opioid use)
15. Polyethylene glycol 17g PO daily PRN constipation

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: See prior notes. Relevant: mCRPC (as above), hypertension (well-controlled on current regimen prior to oncology), prior Grade 3 anemia requiring transfusions (on olaparib), prior Grade 3 neutropenia (Cycle 1 carboplatin/docetaxel). No known cardiac disease. No diabetes.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs: Temperature 97.9°F, Heart Rate 94 bpm (regular), Blood Pressure 108/68 mmHg, Respiratory Rate 16, SpO2 96% on room air. Weight: 168.2 lbs (down from 172.0 lbs at last visit, 3 weeks ago). Height: 5'9". BMI: 24.8.

General: Alert, oriented x3. Appears fatigued and mildly cachectic. Seated comfortably in exam chair. Wearing knit cap due to alopecia. In no acute distress at rest but visibly exerted after transferring from wheelchair to exam table.

HEENT: Atraumatic. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis. No oral thrush. Mild conjunctival pallor noted. No scleral icterus.

Neck: No lymphadenopathy. No JVD.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ throughout. No lower extremity edema.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles. No dullness to percussion. Breathing unlabored at rest.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant (less pronounced than prior visits -- patient notes it is "much better" than before chemotherapy). No guarding or rigidity. Liver edge palpable approximately 2 cm below right costal margin (borderline, note hepatic metastases). Spleen not palpable. No ascites.

Musculoskeletal: Mild tenderness with palpation over lumbar spine (L3-L4 region, consistent with known bone metastases). Tenderness over right hip girdle. No obvious swelling of joints. ROM of lower extremities mildly limited by pain and deconditioning.

Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Strength 4/5 in bilateral lower extremities (iliopsoas and quadriceps mildly weak, likely from deconditioning and neuropathy). Bilateral stocking-distribution sensory loss to light touch and proprioception to the mid-calf. New: sensory loss to light touch at fingertips bilaterally (glove distribution beginning). Absent Achilles reflexes bilaterally. Romberg positive. Gait is unsteady; patient requires assistance for ambulation. Fine motor testing: difficulty with finger-thumb opposition bilaterally.

Skin: Diffuse alopecia (Grade 2). No rash. No evidence of bleeding or petechiae. No jaundice. IV access site without signs of infection.

Lymph nodes: No palpable cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy appreciated on exam.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (drawn today):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 6.8 K/uL (normal, on filgrastim support -- last filgrastim approximately 4 days ago)
- ANC: 4.1 K/uL
- Hemoglobin: 9.0 g/dL (Grade 2 anemia, down from 9.2 at last visit)
- Hematocrit: 27.4%
- MCV: 101 fL (macrocytic, consistent with prior darbepoetin use and olaparib history)
- Platelets: 98 K/uL (Grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia, trending down from 102 K/uL last visit)
- Reticulocyte count: 1.2% (mildly improved from prior, likely darbepoetin effect)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 136 mEq/L
- Potassium: 3.8 mEq/L
- Chloride: 101 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 22 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 1.3 mg/dL (eGFR ~52 mL/min/1.73m2, stable)
- Glucose: 102 mg/dL
- Total protein: 6.1 g/dL (mildly low)
- Albumin: 2.9 g/dL (low, nutritional compromise)
- Total bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL
- ALT: 61 U/L (mildly elevated, up from 48 U/L; possible hepatic metastasis-related)
- AST: 68 U/L (mildly elevated, up from 52 U/L)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 148 U/L (elevated, up from 112 U/L -- likely bony and hepatic contribution)
- LDH: 312 U/L (elevated)

Endocrine / Tumor Markers:
- PSA: 19.8 ng/mL (RISING -- up from 12.1 ng/mL after Cycle 3, suggesting loss of response; prior pre-chemo PSA was 31.7 ng/mL at carboplatin/docetaxel initiation)
- Testosterone: <10 ng/dL (castrate level confirmed)

Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1 (normal). PTT 32 sec.

Magnesium: 1.8 mg/dL (low-normal).

---

IMAGING:
Restaging imaging has been ordered and is pending at the time of this visit (PSMA PET/CT and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis). Results are not yet available; imaging is scheduled for later this week. This visit assessment is based on clinical and laboratory data; a separate imaging results discussion will follow once scans are reviewed.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER (mCRPC), BRCA2 biallelic loss, progressive disease:

Mr. Kowalski has completed 4 cycles of carboplatin/docetaxel (dose-reduced docetaxel 55 mg/m2, carboplatin AUC 4). After initial encouraging PSA decline and partial hepatic response (confirmed at 2-cycle restaging CT), his PSA has now risen from 12.1 to 19.8 ng/mL -- a concerning upward trend that strongly suggests loss of chemotherapy efficacy. This is consistent with the aggressive biology of his tumor (AR amplification, TP53 and RB1 loss, PTEN loss in addition to BRCA2 biallelic loss), which portends a short-lived response window even to platinum-based therapy.

I have discussed the PSA trajectory with Mr. Kowalski and his family at length today. Restaging imaging has been ordered; results are anticipated within the next 3-4 days and we will review them together in a scheduled phone call or brief office visit. If imaging confirms radiographic progression -- which the clinical trajectory and PSA kinetics make likely -- we will need to discuss next therapeutic steps.

Treatment options reviewed in detail with patient and family today:

a) Lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Lu-177-PSMA-617; Pluvicto): This is our primary consideration and the option most likely to offer meaningful benefit at this juncture, should imaging confirm PSMA-avid progressive disease. His prior PSMA PET/CT (obtained at time of progression on olaparib, approximately 3 months ago) demonstrated strongly PSMA-avid disease at all metastatic sites with high SUVmax values. All lesions including hepatic and bone were PSMA-avid, making him potentially eligible for Lu-177-PSMA-617 based on VISION trial criteria. We discussed that this is an FDA-approved, nuclear medicine-administered therapy, given IV every 6 weeks for up to 6 cycles, with manageable toxicity (mainly fatigue, dry mouth, nausea, and hematologic effects). We discussed realistic expectations: approximately half of patients see PSA responses, and the therapy can meaningfully extend life and improve bone pain in PSMA-avid disease. HOWEVER, I emphasized that his current performance status (ECOG 2-3 today, functionally requiring assistance with some ADLs) is a critical eligibility consideration that will need to be reassessed after imaging. I have placed a referral to nuclear medicine / theranostics for evaluation pending imaging results.

b) Cabazitaxel (Jevtana): As a third-line taxane (this would technically be his second taxane exposure, having received docetaxel), cabazitaxel is an option for mCRPC post-docetaxel. However, given his current ECOG PS of 2-3, cumulative Grade 2-3 peripheral neuropathy (worsening, with new hand involvement and near-fall), ongoing anemia, and thrombocytopenia, the risk-benefit calculus is unfavorable. Cabazitaxel would be expected to worsen neuropathy and myelosuppression substantially. I do not recommend this option at the current time unless performance status improves significantly.

c) Clinical trial: I reviewed our active clinical trial portfolio. There may be opportunities for trials targeting AR-pathway resistance or DNA damage response. I will review available studies and discuss at next visit.

d) Best supportive care and hospice: This option was honestly and compassionately discussed. Given his trajectory -- Stage IV disease with liver and bone involvement, ECOG PS 2-3, progressive disease through four prior treatment lines (ADT+abiraterone, enzalutamide, olaparib, platinum/taxane doublet), falling albumin, significant weight loss, and worsening functional status -- a transition to hospice with comfort-focused care is a medically appropriate and potentially preferred path for some patients. We discussed that hospice does not mean "giving up" but rather focusing on quality of life, symptom management, and being home with family. His family was present and engaged in this conversation. Mr. Kowalski acknowledged understanding and expressed that he wants to see the scan results before making any final decisions. He is not ready for hospice at this time but appreciated the candor.

Plan: Order restaging PSMA PET/CT + CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (ordered today). Carboplatin/docetaxel held pending imaging given PSA re-elevation and ECOG decline. Referral to nuclear medicine/theranostics for Lu-177-PSMA-617 evaluation placed. Follow-up call scheduled in 3-4 days for imaging results review. Goals of care conversation documented; advance directive discussion ongoing.

2. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (Grade 2-3, cumulative taxane-related):
The patient now has significant bilateral stocking-glove neuropathy with impaired balance (positive Romberg, near-fall) and emerging hand dysfunction. This is both a safety concern and a quality-of-life concern. Given that chemotherapy is being held, further taxane-related neuropathy progression should plateau and potentially improve over weeks to months.
- Physical therapy referral renewed (focus on fall prevention, balance training, assistive device assessment)
- Occupational therapy referral for hand function and ADL assistance
- Duloxetine 30 mg PO daily initiated for chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN); plan to uptitrate to 60 mg if tolerated at 2 weeks -- evidence base modest but reasonable option for symptom management
- Safety counseling: patient instructed to use walker/cane at all times, remove home fall hazards, install grab bars in bathroom (home health referral placed)
- Gabapentin discussed as alternative agent; deferred for now to avoid additive sedation given opioid use

3. ANEMIA (Grade 2, hemoglobin 9.0 g/dL):
Anemia is multifactorial: cumulative chemotherapy effect, darbepoetin alfa support ongoing, possible component from hepatic metastases and poor nutritional status (albumin 2.9). Hemoglobin has dropped slightly from 9.2 to 9.0. No active transfusion indication today (no symptomatic hypoxia, not meeting threshold for routine transfusion in this context).
- Continue darbepoetin alfa 500 mcg SC every 3 weeks per current schedule
- Repeat CBC in 2 weeks
- Transfusion threshold: will transfuse if Hgb <8.0 g/dL or sooner if symptomatic
- Iron studies to be rechecked today to ensure no superimposed iron deficiency (ordered)

4. THROMBOCYTOPENIA (Grade 1-2, platelets 98 K/uL):
Trending down gradually. No bleeding symptoms currently. Given chemotherapy hold, expect gradual platelet recovery.
- Monitor closely with repeat CBC in 2 weeks
- Counsel patient on bleeding precautions (no NSAIDs, minimize trauma)
- Thrombocytopenia threshold for transfusion: <10K (or <20K if febrile)

5. HEPATIC METASTASES / LIVER FUNCTION:
Mildly rising transaminases (ALT 61, AST 68, ALP 148) from last visit likely reflecting hepatic tumor burden (hepatic segment VII lesion previously identified, with concern for progression given PSA rise). LDH 312 elevated. Bilirubin normal. Will review hepatic lesion behavior on pending imaging.
- Monitor LFTs and bilirubin closely
- If hepatic failure develops, may become a palliative/supportive care priority

6. BONE METASTASES / PAIN MANAGEMENT:
Ongoing skeletal involvement at multiple sites. Pain partially controlled on current regimen.
- Denosumab (Xgeva) continued per schedule
- Pain regimen: scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg TID continued; hydrocodone/acetaminophen PRN continued. If pain escalates, will consider escalation to oxycodone or morphine extended-release in coordination with palliative care.
- Palliative care team actively co-managing; next palliative care appointment in 1 week
- Counseled patient to report any new or escalating pain, particularly any radicular symptoms or bowel/bladder changes (spinal cord compression precautions given multiple vertebral lesions)

7. NUTRITIONAL STATUS:
Albumin 2.9 g/dL, continued weight loss. Reduced appetite.
- Dietary consultation placed
- High-calorie, high-protein oral supplements recommended (Ensure Plus or similar, 2-3 per day)
- Appetite stimulants discussed: megestrol acetate was considered but not initiated given potential for fluid retention and thromboembolic risk, and limited meaningful benefit in advanced cancer setting; mirtazapine 7.5 mg QHS initiated as modest appetite stimulant/antiemetic with the additional benefit of sleep support

8. LEUPROLIDE (ADT) CONTINUATION:
Continuing leuprolide 22.5 mg IM every 3 months for testosterone suppression. Next injection due in approximately 6 weeks; scheduled in the system. Testosterone confirmed castrate today (<10 ng/dL). ADT maintained as backbone regardless of subsequent therapy decisions.

9. GOALS OF CARE / ADVANCE CARE PLANNING:
Extensive family meeting held today (approximately 45 minutes) including Mr. Kowalski, his daughter (primary caregiver), and son-in-law. Discussed disease trajectory, realistic prognosis, burdens of further therapy, and patient values. Patient and family understand disease is advanced and that options are narrowing. Healthcare proxy (daughter) identified and documented. Patient has completed POLST form (updated today: desires full resuscitation at present, does not wish for prolonged mechanical ventilation if no reasonable expectation of meaningful recovery). Hospice conversation ongoing; patient open to revisiting after imaging. Social work involved for family support and home health coordination.

FOLLOW-UP:
- Phone call or brief office visit in 3-4 days for imaging results review and treatment decision
- Return to clinic in 2-3 weeks for CBC recheck, clinical assessment, and treatment planning
- Palliative care: 1 week
- Nuclear medicine/theranostics evaluation: pending, contingent on imaging

Time spent in face-to-face counseling and care coordination: approximately 55 minutes of a 60-minute visit.

Electronically signed,
Attending Medical Oncologist
